prequiz.title: How HTTP Works
prequiz.subtitle: Key terms before you start
prequiz.description: Review these concepts before tackling the HTTP quiz. Understanding them will help you answer questions about request methods, status codes, headers, and protocol versions.

keywords.definition: HyperText Transfer Protocol — the application-layer protocol used to transfer data on the Web. It is stateless and text-based, running over TCP on port 80 by default.
A message sent by a client (usually a browser) to a server asking for a resource or submitting data. It includes a method, URL, headers, and optionally a body.
The server's reply to an HTTP request. It includes a status line (status code + reason phrase), headers, and optionally a body.
Verbs that indicate the desired action: GET (retrieve), POST (submit/create), PUT (replace), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove), HEAD (headers only), OPTIONS (capabilities).
Three-digit numbers in responses indicating outcome: 1xx informational, 2xx success (200 OK), 3xx redirection (301, 302), 4xx client errors (400, 401, 403, 404), 5xx server errors (500, 503).
Key-value pairs in requests and responses that carry metadata: Content-Type, Cache-Control, Authorization, Accept, Set-Cookie, etc.
A feature introduced in HTTP/1.1 where a single TCP connection is reused for multiple request-response cycles, reducing connection overhead. Requests are still sequential.
An HTTP/2 feature that allows multiple requests and responses to be sent concurrently over a single TCP connection using streams, eliminating head-of-line blocking at the HTTP layer.
An HTTP method is idempotent if making the same request multiple times has the same effect as making it once. GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, and OPTIONS are idempotent. POST is not.

An HTTP header that controls how and for how long responses are cached. Key directives: no-store (never cache), no-cache (revalidate before use), max-age (seconds until stale), private (browser only).
